quiz_questions.question_text: Which point is a solution to the system: y > x and y < 5?
quiz_questions.explanation: Check (2, 4): 4 > 2 is true and 4 < 5 is true. Both inequalities are satisfied.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is (1, 3) a solution to: y ≥ 2x + 1 and y ≤ -x + 6?
quiz_questions.explanation: First: 3 ≥ 2(1) + 1 = 3, so 3 ≥ 3 is true. Second: 3 ≤ -(1) + 6 = 5, so 3 ≤ 5 is true. Both are satisfied.

quiz_questions.question_text: The solution region of a system of two linear inequalities is:

options.text: The area where the shaded regions of both inequalities overlap
The line between the two inequalities

quiz_questions.explanation: The solution to a system of inequalities is the region where the individual solutions overlap—any point in this region satisfies all inequalities.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which point is NOT a solution to y > 0 and x > 0?
quiz_questions.explanation: (-2, 3): x = -2 is not greater than 0, so this point fails the second inequality.

quiz_questions.question_text: For the system y ≤ x + 4 and y ≥ -x, should the boundary lines be solid or dashed?
quiz_questions.explanation: Both inequalities use ≤ and ≥ (inclusive), so both boundary lines are solid.

quiz_questions.question_text: A system of two linear inequalities has no overlapping region. How many solutions does the system have?
quiz_questions.explanation: If the shaded regions of the two inequalities do not overlap, there is no point that satisfies both, meaning the system has no solution.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which inequality has a dashed boundary line?
quiz_questions.explanation: A dashed line is used for strict inequalities (< or >). Since y > 2x - 5 uses >, its boundary is dashed.

quiz_questions.question_text: For the system y < 3 and x > -1, in which quadrants does most of the solution region lie?

options.text: Quadrants I and IV, and part of II

quiz_questions.explanation: y < 3 includes everything below the line y = 3. x > -1 includes everything right of x = -1. The overlap extends through Quadrants I, IV, and part of II.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is (0, 0) a solution to: y ≥ x - 2 and y ≤ x + 3?
quiz_questions.explanation: First: 0 ≥ 0 - 2 = -2, so 0 ≥ -2 is true. Second: 0 ≤ 0 + 3 = 3, so 0 ≤ 3 is true. (0, 0) is a solution.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which system has a solution region that forms a triangle with the x-axis?
quiz_questions.explanation: The three inequalities form boundaries: y = x + 4, y = -x + 4, and y = 0 (x-axis). Their overlap creates a triangular region.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'system of inequalities'?

options.text: Two equations set equal to each other
Two or more inequalities considered together, with the solution being the region that satisfies all of them
A single inequality with two variables
A compound inequality on a number line

quiz_questions.explanation: A system of inequalities consists of two or more inequalities. The solution is the set of all points that satisfy every inequality in the system.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'feasible region' mean?

options.text: A single line on the graph
The region on a graph where all inequalities in the system are satisfied
The intersection point of two lines

quiz_questions.explanation: The feasible region is the area on the graph where all the constraints (inequalities) overlap, representing all possible solutions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'boundary line' mean in graphing inequalities?

options.text: The line that separates the solution region from the non-solution region

quiz_questions.explanation: A boundary line is the graph of the related equation (replacing the inequality sign with =). It divides the plane into the solution half and the non-solution half.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'solid line' represent when graphing an inequality?

options.text: Points on the line are not included
Points on the line are included in the solution (≤ or ≥)

quiz_questions.explanation: A solid boundary line means the points on the line are part of the solution, used with ≤ or ≥.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'dashed line' represent when graphing an inequality?

options.text: Points on the line are not included in the solution (< or >)
Points on the line are included

quiz_questions.explanation: A dashed boundary line means the points on the line are not part of the solution, used with strict inequalities (< or >).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'half-plane' mean?

options.text: The area above the x-axis only
One of the two regions that a line divides the coordinate plane into

quiz_questions.explanation: A line divides the coordinate plane into two half-planes. A linear inequality shades one of these half-planes as its solution.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'test point' mean when graphing inequalities?

options.text: The y-intercept of the boundary line
A point (usually the origin) used to determine which side of the boundary line to shade
Any point on the boundary line
The point where two lines intersect

quiz_questions.explanation: A test point (often (0,0) if it's not on the boundary) is plugged into the inequality. If it makes the inequality true, shade that side; if false, shade the other side.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'shading' represent on the graph of an inequality?

options.text: Points that do not satisfy the inequality
All the points that satisfy the inequality

quiz_questions.explanation: Shading indicates the region of all ordered pairs (x, y) that make the inequality true.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'overlapping region' mean in a system of inequalities?

options.text: The area where the shaded regions of all inequalities intersect

quiz_questions.explanation: The overlapping region is where the shaded areas of all individual inequalities meet. Points in this region satisfy every inequality in the system.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'constraint' mean in the context of systems of inequalities?

options.text: A limitation or condition expressed as an inequality
The origin of the coordinate plane

quiz_questions.explanation: A constraint is a condition or limitation, often expressed as an inequality, that the variables must satisfy.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student must study at least 2 hours of math (m ≥ 2) and at least 1 hour of science (s ≥ 1), but no more than 6 hours total (m + s ≤ 6). If she studies 3 hours of math, what is the range of science hours she can study?
quiz_questions.explanation: With m = 3: s ≥ 1 and 3 + s ≤ 6, so s ≤ 3. The range is 1 ≤ s ≤ 3.

quiz_questions.question_text: A bakery makes cakes (c) and pies (p). Each cake requires 3 hours and each pie requires 2 hours. The baker has at most 24 hours available (3c + 2p ≤ 24) and must make at least 2 of each (c ≥ 2, p ≥ 2). What is the maximum number of cakes if exactly 2 pies are made?
quiz_questions.explanation: With p = 2: 3c + 4 ≤ 24, 3c ≤ 20, c ≤ 6.67. Since c must be a whole number, c ≤ 6. Maximum is 6 cakes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A farmer grows corn (x acres) and soybeans (y acres). She has at most 100 acres (x + y ≤ 100), must plant at least 20 acres of corn (x ≥ 20), and at least 10 acres of soybeans (y ≥ 10). Is planting 80 acres of corn and 25 acres of soybeans feasible?

options.text: Yes, because both minimums are met
No, because corn must be less than 50

quiz_questions.explanation: Check: x ≥ 20 (80 ≥ 20, true), y ≥ 10 (25 ≥ 10, true), but x + y ≤ 100 (105 ≤ 100, false). It exceeds the acreage limit.

quiz_questions.question_text: A school orders buses (b) and vans (v) for a field trip. Each bus holds 40 students and each van holds 12. They need to transport at least 200 students (40b + 12v ≥ 200). Buses cost $300 and vans cost $100. The budget is at most $1,500 (300b + 100v ≤ 1500). Which option works?
quiz_questions.explanation: 3 buses + 7 vans: Capacity = 120 + 84 = 204 ≥ 200. Cost = 900 + 700 = $1,600... Let me recheck. Actually 300(3) + 100(7) = 900 + 700 = 1600 > 1500. Let me try 4 buses, 3 vans: 160 + 36 = 196 < 200. Try 3 buses, 7 vans: over budget. Try 4 buses, 2 vans: 160+24=184<200. 3 buses, 8 vans: 120+96=216≥200, cost=900+800=1700>1500. Let's try 5 buses, 0 vans: 200≥200, cost=1500≤1500. This works!

quiz_questions.question_text: A pet store can hold at most 30 animals. They must have at least 5 cats (c ≥ 5) and at least 5 dogs (d ≥ 5), with c + d ≤ 30. If the store has 12 cats, how many dogs can they have?
quiz_questions.explanation: With c = 12: d ≥ 5 and 12 + d ≤ 30, so d ≤ 18. The range is 5 ≤ d ≤ 18.

quiz_questions.question_text: A nutritionist requires a meal to have at least 20g of protein (p ≥ 20) and at most 50g of carbs (c ≤ 50). A chicken salad has 30g protein and 25g carbs. Does it meet the requirements?
quiz_questions.explanation: 30 ≥ 20 (true) and 25 ≤ 50 (true). Both constraints are satisfied.

quiz_questions.question_text: A concert venue has floor seats (f) and balcony seats (b). The venue holds at most 500 people (f + b ≤ 500). Floor seats cost $75 and balcony seats cost $50. The venue wants at least $30,000 in revenue (75f + 50b ≥ 30000). What is the minimum floor seats needed if all 500 seats are filled?

A factory makes widgets (w) and gadgets (g). Each widget takes 2 hours and each gadget takes 3 hours. There are 60 hours available (2w + 3g ≤ 60). Each widget earns $5 and each gadget earns $8. If the factory makes 15 widgets, at most how many gadgets can it make?

A student buys notebooks (n at $3 each) and pens (p at $1 each). She has at most $15 to spend (3n + p ≤ 15) and needs at least 2 notebooks (n ≥ 2) and at least 3 pens (p ≥ 3). If she buys 3 notebooks, what is the maximum number of pens?
quiz_questions.explanation: 3(3) + p ≤ 15, 9 + p ≤ 15, p ≤ 6. Maximum 6 pens (and p ≥ 3 is satisfied).

quiz_questions.question_text: A gardener has space for at most 50 plants. She wants at least 15 roses (r ≥ 15) and at least 10 tulips (t ≥ 10), with r + t ≤ 50. She also wants at least twice as many roses as tulips (r ≥ 2t). If she plants exactly 10 tulips, what is the range of roses she can plant?
quiz_questions.explanation: With t = 10: r ≥ 15 (constraint 1), r ≥ 2(10) = 20 (constraint 4), r + 10 ≤ 50 so r ≤ 40 (constraint 3). The binding lower bound is r ≥ 20, so 20 ≤ r ≤ 40.
